coverage.notes: Descriptive work metadata is added only after source-specific cataloging. A repository work absent from this index has not yet been cataloged in this system; absence is not a claim that metadata is unknowable.

items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter; from John of Hildesheim's Historia trium regum
items.date_display: Conventionally dated around 1400; securely after the 1364-1375 Latin source and before the dated 1442 English witness
items.region_display: England; exact place of translation not securely established

items.preferred_title: The Thirde Order of Seynt Franceys for the Brethren and Susters of the Order of Penitentis
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English translator; Latin source promulgated by Nicholas IV
items.date_display: Translation undated; attested in a manuscript of around 1500
items.region_display: England; exact place of translation and copying unknown

items.date_display: Translated or adapted c. 1400
items.region_display: England; rhyme-based Northern English dialect localization; narrative set in Ireland

items.date_display: Composed during the reign of Edward III and completed 15 May 1338
items.region_display: Completed at Sixhills, Lincolnshire, England; full composition geography not established

items.author_display: Various named and anonymous documentary creators
items.date_display: Documents dated or assigned c. 1290–11 October 1483; edited selection first published 1919
items.region_display: England, especially Stonor and the family's Oxfordshire and Thames Valley networks

items.date_display: Probably second half of the thirteenth century; one corpus proposes 1260–1290
items.region_display: England; original dialect and place of composition unresolved

items.preferred_title: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English translator-redactor; traditionally attributed to the fictional or legendary narrator John Mandeville
items.date_display: Around 1400; uniquely attested in Egerton MS 1982
items.region_display: England; the unique witness's scribal language is localized to the North Riding of Yorkshire

items.date_display: Conventionally dated c. 1400; attested in a manuscript copied in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1842
items.region_display: England; the sole witness's scribal language is most likely Huntingdonshire in the southern Central Midlands

items.date_display: Probably later thirteenth century; traditionally dated c. 1225, with the 1270s proposed as a plausible estimate
items.region_display: Southern or southeastern England; exact place of composition disputed

items.date_display: Late fourteenth century; uniquely attested in a manuscript copied 1375–1400
items.region_display: England; poet's dialect associated with Staffordshire, while the unique witness's scribal dialect is South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire

items.preferred_title: An English Chronicle of the Reigns of Richard II, Henry IV, Henry V, and Henry VI
items.author_display: Anonymous compilers; Richard Fox and an anonymous Eulogium compiler for appended selections
items.date_display: Main continuation compiled in stages and completed after 4 March 1461, probably before 22 May 1471; Fox source completed 1448; first published 1856
items.region_display: England; St Albans for the Richard Fox component; main continuation's exact place unresolved

items.date_display: Probably c. 1478–1483; first known in Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19 and printed by about 1494
items.region_display: London, England (scholarly localization)

items.date_display: c. 1175; autograph witness dated 1170–1185
items.region_display: East Midlands; Bourne Abbey in south Lincolnshire has been proposed

items.date_display: Late fourteenth century, probably around 1400; sole surviving copy made c. 1430–1450
items.region_display: Probably the North Midlands of England; sole surviving copy associated with Robert Thornton of East Newton, Yorkshire

items.date_display: Fifteenth century; conventionally dated c. 1440-c. 1450
items.region_display: Northern England; the Ribble Valley and Salley (Sawley) Abbey are a later scholarly proposal

items.preferred_title: Treatises of Fistula in Ano, Haemorrhoids, and Clysters
items.author_display: John Arderne as author of the Latin source treatises; anonymous Middle English translators
items.date_display: Latin fistula treatise dated 1376; main English translation after 1413 and attested in Sloane MS 6, 1425-1449
items.region_display: England; exact place and dialect of translation unresolved

items.editor_display: Richard Morris; newly collated and corrected by Pamela Gradon
items.date_display: Completed 27 October 1340; first printed 1866
items.region_display: Kentish Middle English; autograph produced at St Augustine's Abbey, Canterbury

items.author_display: Anonymous French prose adapter; translated and adapted by William Caxton
items.date_display: Translated and first printed c. 1489
items.region_display: England; first printed at Westminster

items.author_display: Multiple anonymous and named documentary creators
items.editor_display: John H. Fisher, Malcolm Richardson, and Jane L. Fisher
items.date_display: Selected records currently corrected to 1384–1455 (anthology/XML headings report 1384–1462); anthology published 1984
items.region_display: England, especially institutions associated with London and Westminster, with wider British and continental connections

items.preferred_title: The Lyf of the Noble and Crysten Prynce, Charles the Grete
items.author_display: Jean Bagnyon; translated and adapted by William Caxton
items.date_display: Translation completed 18 June 1485; first printed 1 December 1485
items.region_display: England; first printed at Westminster

items.author_display: Geoffrey Chaucer; supplementary conclusions of uncertain authorship
items.date_display: Core dated 1391; additions dated 1393 or later
items.region_display: England; exact place of composition not securely established

items.author_display: Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius; translated and adapted by Geoffrey Chaucer
items.date_display: Translated around 1380
items.region_display: England; exact place of translation not securely established

items.date_display: Composed c. 1385
items.region_display: England; exact place of composition not securely established; narrative set in Troy

items.author_display: Thomas N. (translator of Aelred of Rievaulx; identity uncertain)
items.date_display: Fourteenth century; necessarily before the Vernon copy of 1381-1400
items.region_display: Translation region unknown; the surviving copy has a West Midland surface layer over mixed earlier dialect strata

items.preferred_title: Kleine Publicationen aus der Auchinleck-Hs. I–IX
items.editor_display: Eugen Kölbing; Max Schwarz; F. Krause
items.date_display: Medieval texts attested from c. 1331–1340 onward; source installments first published 1884–1888
items.region_display: England; exact medieval composition places vary or remain unresolved

items.date_display: Compiled after 1467 according to Napier; surviving manuscript dated c. 1480-1500 by the Fitzwilliam Museum
items.region_display: England; exact place of compilation and dialect region unresolved

items.preferred_title: A Chronicle of London, from 1089 to 1483
items.author_display: Anonymous London chronicle compilers; John Lydgate and John Bocking for identified or attributed components
items.editor_display: Nicholas Harris Nicolas and Edward Tyrrell
items.date_display: London Chronicle compiled in stages in the early fifteenth century and continued to 1483; edited compilation first published 1827
items.region_display: England, especially London; exact place of compilation is not established for every component

items.preferred_title: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard
items.date_display: By the late fourteenth century; exact composition date unknown
items.region_display: England; Kribel tentatively reconstructed a southern Midlands dialect near Kent, but surviving witness dialects are geographically diverse

items.date_display: c. 1445; composed within the broader range 1443-1449
items.region_display: Mainly East Midland language; exact place of composition unresolved

items.date_display: Late fourteenth century; uniquely attested in a manuscript copied 1446-1460
items.region_display: Composition place uncertain: Northeast Midlands or East Anglia; the unique copy is assigned to the South-East or South-East Midlands

items.preferred_title: The Middle-English Harrowing of Hell and Gospel of Nicodemus
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English poets and translator-adapters
items.date_display: Harrowing composed c. 1200–1250; Gospel composed c. 1300–1350; edition first published 1907
items.region_display: England; the Gospel's authorial dialect is Northern English

items.date_display: Traditionally assigned to the early fifteenth century; exact composition and earliest-copy dates unresolved
items.region_display: Place of composition unknown; the Harley copy is from England and the Lansdowne miscellany is associated with London

items.preferred_title: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century
items.editor_display: George Henslow, with commentary by Walter W. Skeat
items.date_display: Medieval source material, later fourteenth to fifteenth centuries, with unresolved dating for manuscript A; edition published 1899
items.region_display: England; more specific composition places are uncertain and witness-specific dialect claims are not generalized to the whole selection

items.date_display: Composition date unknown; attested in a manuscript portion dated to the second half of the fifteenth century and published by Horstmann in 1885
items.region_display: England-associated Middle English text; exact place of composition unknown; routes extend from England and Flanders through the Mediterranean to Egypt, the Levant, Rhodes, and Constantinople

items.author_display: Christine de Pizan; translated by Stephen Scrope
items.date_display: Translated around 1440; first printed from Longleat House MS 253 in 1904
items.region_display: England; exact place of translation unknown

items.preferred_title: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
items.author_display: Anonymous medieval authors; the main paraphrase is conventionally attributed to Thomas Brampton
items.date_display: Early fifteenth century for the main paraphrase; later annotations give 1413 and 1414; edition published 1842
items.region_display: England; no single more precise composition place is established for all three parts

items.preferred_title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)
items.date_display: Composed 1350–1400; attested by a late-fourteenth-century manuscript and first known in print in Wright's 1845 text issue
items.region_display: Middle English work with an English Midlands dialect association; exact place of composition unknown; frame narrative set in Rome

items.preferred_title: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose
items.author_display: Anonymous English authors or adapters of two distinct textual versions
items.date_display: Metrical Life composed 1250-1290; selected prose source printed 27 August 1527; edited selection published 1844
items.region_display: England; no single more precise place of composition is established for both texts

items.date_display: Composed c. 1385
items.region_display: England; exact place of composition not securely established; narrative set in Troy

items.preferred_title: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester (long recension)
items.author_display: Robert of Gloucester (attributed; identity and extent of responsibility uncertain)
items.date_display: Long recension after 1297, perhaps after 1307; exact composition history uncertain
items.region_display: Middle English work associated linguistically with the Worcestershire–Gloucestershire area; exact place of composition unknown

items.date_display: Revised after 1457 and still in progress in or after September 1464; unfinished
items.region_display: England; exact place or places of second-version revision not securely established

items.preferred_title: Letters and Papers of John Shillingford, Mayor of Exeter, 1447–50
items.author_display: John Shillingford and other named and anonymous civic, ecclesiastical, and administrative documentary creators
items.date_display: Historical documents c. 1443–1449, chiefly late 1447–spring 1448; edited selection first published 1871
items.region_display: Exeter and Devon, England, with civic business in London

items.editor_display: Eugen Kölbing; Joseph Hall collated Ipomadon A against its manuscript
items.date_display: A: late 14th–mid-15th century; B: second half of the 15th century; C: 15th century; edition published 1889
items.region_display: England; A is localized broadly to the North Midlands and B to the Northeast Midlands, while C's compositional locality remains unresolved

items.date_display: Underlying romances composed before 1320 through the early fifteenth century; edited selection published 1844
items.region_display: Northeast Midlands of England, with Eglamour perhaps associated more specifically with Yorkshire; witness copying and publication places are recorded separately

items.date_display: Completed 1 December 1422
items.region_display: England; ancient Rome as historical setting

items.author_display: Anonymous Dominican compiler of King's Lynn; Richard Fraunces tentatively proposed; older Geoffrey the Grammarian attribution disputed
items.date_display: Dated 1440 in the Harley redaction; first printed 15 May 1499
items.region_display: King's Lynn and Norfolk, England; the compiler explicitly followed Norfolk usage, while the Harley base witness is probably not East Anglian

items.date_display: Underlying cycle probably composed c. 1295-1306; edited selection published 1862
items.region_display: Northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; witness copying and Edinburgh publication are recorded separately

items.preferred_title: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume II
items.date_display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; Arnold selection published 1871
items.region_display: England; Wycliffite or Lollard textual culture

items.preferred_title: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume III: Miscellaneous Works
items.author_display: John Wyclif, traditionally attributed; Arnold identifies Rolle-derived material and anonymous Wycliffite or Lollard writing, and several attributions are disputed or rejected
items.date_display: Individual medieval composition dates remain under review; no. XXIX dates to 1388–1389; edition published 1871
items.region_display: England; Oxford publication imprint

items.author_display: Thomas Usk; John Gower; Thomas Hoccleve; Henry Scogan; John Lydgate; Sir Richard Roos; John Walton; Sir John Clanvowe (attributed); and anonymous writers
items.date_display: Works composed from about 1387 through the late fifteenth century; edition published 1897
items.region_display: England, with two short selected pieces transmitted in a late Scottish witness in Older Scots form

items.preferred_title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)
items.date_display: Composed by about 1350, perhaps in the first half of the fourteenth century; attested in a mid- to late-fourteenth-century witness; portions first printed in 1810 and the complete Northern text in 1907
items.region_display: Middle English work with a Northern English dialect association; exact place of composition is unknown; frame narrative set in Rome

items.preferred_title: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies: selected vocabularies XIII–XX
items.date_display: Components transmitted from the early thirteenth through the fifteenth/early sixteenth century; selected second-edition compilation published 1884
items.region_display: England; component witnesses and dialect associations include Worcester, Reading, Shropshire, Lincolnshire, and tentative western-border and northern areas

items.author_display: Various medieval writers, principally anonymous; Saint Godric and Henry II are associated with individual pieces
items.date_display: Texts selected as examples of approximately 1130–1250; edition compiled and published 1920
items.region_display: England; the pieces and witnesses represent multiple regional and dialectal settings rather than one place of composition

items.author_display: Geoffrey Chaucer (Fragment A, almost certainly); anonymous or disputed translators (Fragments B–C)
items.date_display: Fragment A often dated to the 1360s and before 1372; Fragments B–C undated here; first printed in 1532
items.region_display: England; Fragment B has Northern English dialect features

items.date_display: Probably composed in the early fourteenth century; attested in early-fourteenth-century and circa 1330–1340 witnesses; independently printed in 1896, with the immediate completed edition published in 1898
items.region_display: Middle English work of English cultural origin; exact place of composition is unknown; Morrill's Southern-Midland dialect localization is a historical hypothesis

items.preferred_title: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]
items.author_display: Anonymous Syon brother; Thomas Fishbourne has been proposed
items.date_display: Composed after Syon's 1415 foundation, probably in the first half of the fifteenth century; first printed 1530; source edition 1873
items.region_display: Syon Abbey, Isleworth, Middlesex, England

items.preferred_title: The Lay Folks’ Catechism: 1901 parallel-text collection
items.author_display: John de Gaitrik; John Thoresby; John Peckham; anonymous catechetical compiler
items.editor_display: Thomas Frederick Simmons and Henry Edward Nolloth
items.date_display: 1281 and 1357 medieval components; Lambeth compilation date unresolved; edition published 1901
items.region_display: England, especially York/Yorkshire and Lambeth; London publication imprint

items.preferred_title: How the Wise Man Taught His Son
items.date_display: Fifteenth century; first known witness conventionally dated about 1430; source edition 1889
items.region_display: England; precise place of composition unknown

items.date_display: Composed in the late fourteenth century; attested in a circa 1440-1450 manuscript; first printed in 1865
items.region_display: English Midlands

items.preferred_title: The Prologue and Tale of Beryn
items.editor_display: Frederick James Furnivall and Walter George Stone
items.date_display: Early fifteenth century; component dates uncertain
items.region_display: England; strong Canterbury associations, composition locale uncertain

items.date_display: Probably c. 1500; unique witness late fifteenth century or c. 1500
items.region_display: England; Northeast Midlands?

items.preferred_title: Extracts from the Account Rolls of the Abbey of Durham, Volume I
items.author_display: Durham Cathedral Priory office-holders and accounting clerks
items.date_display: Source records c. 1290–1535 under current cataloging; edited compilation first published 1898
items.region_display: Durham Cathedral Priory and its estates in northern England

items.preferred_title: Extracts from the Account Rolls of the Abbey of Durham, Volume II
items.author_display: Durham Cathedral Priory office-holders and accounting clerks
items.date_display: Source records 1278–1538; edited compilation first published 1899
items.region_display: Durham Cathedral Priory and its estates in northern England

items.preferred_title: Extracts from the Account Rolls of the Abbey of Durham, Volume III
items.author_display: Durham Cathedral Priory office-holders and Durham Cathedral accounting clerks
items.date_display: Source records 1293–1597; edited compilation first published 1901
items.region_display: Durham Cathedral Priory, Durham Cathedral, and their estates in northern England

items.date_display: Probably composed circa 1300; first securely attested in the Auchinleck Manuscript in the 1330s; first surviving print dated 1509; immediate critical edition published in 1913
items.region_display: Middle English work of English cultural origin; London is a tentative rather than secure composition localization

items.preferred_title: King Horn; Floriz and Blauncheflur; The Assumption of Our Lady
items.author_display: Three anonymous medieval poets or translators; no shared authorship
items.editor_display: George H. McKnight, revising Joseph Rawson Lumby
items.date_display: Works composed c. 1200–1290; collection first published 1866 and re-edited 1901
items.region_display: England; proposed origins vary by work from southern and southeastern England to the East Midlands

items.date_display: Composed c. 1421-1422; first printed c. 1497
items.region_display: England; Thebes and the Canterbury return route as narrative settings

items.preferred_title: The Lay Folks Mass Book and offices according to the Use of York (Simmons selection)
items.author_display: Multiple anonymous medieval authors and translators; John Lydgate attributed on selected components
items.date_display: Medieval components attested from the late fourteenth through early sixteenth centuries; source edition published 1879
items.region_display: England, especially York and northern England; LFMB transmission also associated with Derbyshire/Nottinghamshire and Norfolk

items.date_display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; commonly dated around 1400
items.region_display: East Midlands, England

items.date_display: Traditionally dated c. 1462; uniquely attested in a manuscript copy dated to the 1460s
items.region_display: England; precise place of composition unknown

items.date_display: Probably composed 1350-1400; uniquely attested in a manuscript made at Bath c. 1412-1430; first published 1864
items.region_display: England; South-Western Middle English, with the sole witness associated with Bath, Somerset

items.date_display: Toward the end of the fourteenth century (often c. 1400); uniquely attested as a near-complete text in a manuscript copied c. 1470-1480; fragmentarily printed c. 1505 and c. 1510
items.region_display: England; probably the North Midlands, with a more specific northern East Midlands localization proposed but not certain

items.date_display: 1461-1464: Active Policy and Dicta probably 1461-62; A Prisoner's Reflections Michaelmas 1463-24 March 1464
items.region_display: England; Fleet Prison, London, for A Prisoner's Reflections

items.preferred_title: English Prose Treatises of Richard Rolle de Hampole
items.author_display: Richard Rolle (attributed), Walter Hilton, and anonymous or uncertain authors
items.date_display: Fourteenth-century works in late-fourteenth- and fifteenth-century witnesses; first edition 1866, revised thirteen-item reissue 1921
items.region_display: England; principally Northern English witnesses, with a Midland opening supplied for the Mixed Life

items.preferred_title: The Seven Points of True Love and Everlasting Wisdom
items.date_display: Around 1400 or shortly thereafter; certainly available by 1419
items.region_display: England; early transmission associated with Mount Grace Charterhouse, Yorkshire, but place of composition is unconfirmed

items.preferred_title: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English translator/compiler; Latin source authors Philip of Clairvaux, Thomas of Cantimpré, Jacques de Vitry, and Stefano Maconi
items.date_display: Middle English translations produced in the first quarter of the fifteenth century; surviving Douce 114 copy from the first half of the fifteenth century; edited selection published 1885
items.region_display: England; language localized to the Rutland area and broadly Central Midlands, with Beauvale, Nottinghamshire established as later monastic provenance rather than certain production

items.author_display: Anonymous and composite medieval authorship; William Caxton and John Mirk have scoped responsibilities
items.date_display: Medieval components chiefly attested in the fifteenth century; Caxton print 1484; editorial selection published 1880
items.region_display: England and Welsh saint traditions; edited and published at Halle an der Saale, Germany

items.preferred_title: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon
items.date_display: Original Northern Homily Cycle probably composed around 1300 or in the early fourteenth century; Vernon expanded recension copied 1381-1400, commonly c. 1390; edited selection published 1877
items.region_display: Original cycle associated with northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; Vernon copy associated tentatively with the West Midlands; Horstmann's editorial dateline is Sagan (Zagan), 1876

items.author_display: Anonymous medieval authors; John Lydgate (calendar attributed)
items.date_display: late thirteenth to fifteenth century texts; selected editions published 1879–1888
items.region_display: England and Scotland, with part-specific dialect regions

items.preferred_title: Proprium Sanctorum: Supplementary Homilies from the Vernon Manuscript (Horstmann selection)
items.author_display: Anonymous prologue poet and anonymous homilist or homilists
items.date_display: Supplementary homilies composed in the late fourteenth century; parallel Add MS 22283 witness dated 1360-1399; Vernon witness dated 1381-1400, commonly c. 1390; selection published 1888
items.region_display: First Vernon/Simeon expansion transmitted in a Midland dialect; Horstmann tentatively localized the prologue to northwestern England and the remaining additions to southern England

items.preferred_title: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)
items.date_display: Probably around 1300 or the early fourteenth century; earliest surviving exact witness identified here dated 1340-1360; first published in 1877
items.region_display: Northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; surviving copies span northern, Midland, southern, and Irish transmission

items.preferred_title: Zwei mittelenglische Bearbeitungen der Sage von St. Patrik's Purgatorium
items.date_display: Version I attested c. 1331–1340; Version II attested in later fifteenth-century witnesses; edited composite published 1877
items.region_display: England; the precise places of composition are unresolved, while the surviving witnesses belong to distinct London and later medieval English production contexts

items.date_display: Circa 1475; attested in a fifteenth-century manuscript and first known in print in 1909
items.region_display: England; exact composition place unknown

items.preferred_title: King Ponthus and the Fair Sidone
items.date_display: First half of the fifteenth century; attested in the mid-fifteenth-century Digby 185 witness; first published in 1897
items.region_display: England; exact place of translation unresolved

items.preferred_title: The Middle English Metrical Version of the Revelations of Methodius
items.editor_display: Charlotte D'Evelyn; transcript prepared by Marguerite Thiebaud
items.date_display: After 1424, probably fifteenth century; uniquely attested in a fifteenth-century manuscript and first printed in 1918
items.region_display: Probably Norfolk, England, on dialectal evidence; the exact place of composition remains unresolved

items.preferred_title: Poema Morale (McClean 123 version, with opening couplet from Sinners Beware)
items.date_display: Poema Morale: late twelfth century; McClean composite copy: late thirteenth century or c. 1300
items.region_display: England; Poema Morale tentatively localized to the southern South-East Midlands around Middlesex or London

items.preferred_title: Die Katherinenhymne des Ricardus Spaldyng und eine Marienhymne derselben Pergamentrolle
items.author_display: Richard Spaldyng (attributed) and an anonymous poet
items.date_display: Both works are attested in a roll dated 1400–1410; their exact composition dates are unresolved; first printed in 1907
items.region_display: England, with south Lincolnshire authorial and scribal dialect associations; the exact composition places and the meaning of PIPWEL remain unresolved

items.preferred_title: The Destruction of Jerusalem (short version, Pepys 2014)
items.date_display: The wider romance is dated 1375-1400; the short recension is first attested here in a late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century manuscript; first published in two installments, 1903-1904
items.region_display: London for the wider romance; the short recension's separate formation is not securely localized; Pepys MS 2014 was copied in Northamptonshire

items.date_display: Probably composed in the fifteenth century; attested circa 1470; CME source edition published in 1893
items.region_display: England; Norfolk is the probable poet's dialect area, while the surviving witnesses have distinct copying and circulation contexts

items.preferred_title: The Prouerbis of Wysdom (Rawlinson poet. 32 compilation)
items.date_display: Compilation and first known attestation: second half of the fifteenth century (1450–1500); exact composition dates unknown
items.region_display: England; exact compilation place unknown

items.editor_display: Walter Hübner, with textual and linguistic commentary by K. Schreiner
items.date_display: Fifteenth-century composition or compilation; earliest surviving attestation window in the first half of the century; first published in 1911
items.region_display: Northern England for dialect and manuscript tradition; exact place of composition unresolved

items.preferred_title: The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye
items.editor_display: Friedrich Brie; manuscript transcript by Fritz Jung
items.date_display: After 1422 and probably by the mid-fifteenth century; source edition published in 1913
items.region_display: England; the surviving copy's language is localized to Northamptonshire, not necessarily the place of composition

items.preferred_title: Younger Supplementary Legends from MS Bodley 779 (Horstmann selection)
items.date_display: Selected manuscript forms first attested in the second quarter of the fifteenth century (1425–1450); constituent composition dates vary and remain unresolved here
items.region_display: North Hampshire, England, for Bodley 779 compiler orthography; the places of composition of the selected legends are not established

items.preferred_title: An English Friend of Charles of Orléans: Sections B–E
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English poets; William de la Pole, Duke of Suffolk, historically proposed but unconfirmed
items.date_display: Individual composition dates unresolved; surviving witnesses range from the mid-fifteenth into the early sixteenth century; exact assembled selection published 1911
items.region_display: Anglo-French courtly literary networks; exact places of composition unresolved

items.date_display: Late thirteenth century; earliest surviving witness circa 1300; source edition published 1915
items.region_display: Fenland zone of south Lincolnshire, north-west Norfolk, or north-east Cambridgeshire; exact composition place uncertain

items.preferred_title: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)
items.date_display: The poems are tentatively dated by Kaluza to the fourteenth century; surviving medieval witnesses are late-fourteenth- and fifteenth-century; first published separately in 1816 and 1887; Kaluza selection published 1890
items.region_display: English Midlands dialect for both poems; Three Messengers of Death is localized more specifically to the West Midlands

items.preferred_title: Short Pieces from MS. Cotton Galba E. IX
items.author_display: Anonymous authors (four poems; common authorship is not asserted)
items.date_display: Constituent composition dates are mostly unresolved; All Is But a Phantom is estimated c. 1350, the source witness dates 1400-1425, and the exact selection was first published in 1895
items.region_display: Places of composition unresolved; the source witness is associated with Northern England, which is not transferred to the poems' composition

items.preferred_title: The Legend of Theophilus (Rawlinson tail-rhyme version)
items.date_display: Composition not securely dated; sole surviving witness copied in the second half of the fifteenth century; first published in 1903
items.region_display: Scribal language localized to Ely by LALME/eLALME; place of composition unknown

items.preferred_title: The Northern Version of Cato's Distichs
items.date_display: Fourteenth century; earliest-witness dating disputed between the late fourteenth and fifteenth centuries
items.region_display: Northern England by rhyme and vocabulary; surviving witness spellings are mixed

items.author_display: Anonymous authors (four poems; common authorship is not asserted)
items.date_display: Constituent composition dates unresolved; the source witness was made around the beginning of the fifteenth century; Root's exact selection was first published in 1910
items.region_display: Places of composition unresolved; the source witness was made in England, which is not transferred to all four poems' composition

items.preferred_title: Two Tapestry Poems by Lydgate: The Life of St. George and the Falls of Seven Princes (encoded selection)
items.date_display: Sodein Fal: after 1422 and possibly before Lydgate's Fall of Princes; coronation sotelty: 1429; St George poem: possibly 1430; scholarly selection first published December 1910
items.region_display: English cultural origin; London and Westminster are associated civic and courtly contexts, not proven composition places; Hammond's edition was published in Leipzig

items.preferred_title: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version, with Zupitza's textual study)
items.date_display: Poema Morale: late twelfth century; Digby 4 copy: early thirteenth century; first complete Digby edition: 1878
items.region_display: England; original tentatively localized to the southern South-East Midlands around Middlesex or London; Digby copy described as Kentish

items.preferred_title: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan (Horstmann selection)
items.date_display: Both poems predate their late-fourteenth-century witnesses; Horstmann's selected edition was published in 1878
items.region_display: England; dialect evidence has been used to associate Celestin with the East Midlands and Susan with Yorkshire or northern England, but exact places of composition are unknown

items.author_display: Anonymous authors of two verse works
items.date_display: Medieval composition dates unknown; surviving paired witnesses are dated from the later fourteenth century to ca. 1425, with conflicting individual witness dates; Garrett completed the article in 1909 and published it in 1911
items.region_display: England; Add MS 17866 is scribally localized to Lincolnshire, while Takamiya MS 46 is described as East Anglian and by eLALME as southern Lincolnshire; composition place is unknown

items.date_display: First half of the fifteenth century; first known in print circa 1510
items.region_display: England, with a strong London association; exact composition place unknown

items.preferred_title: Saint Antony: Life, Finding, and Translation
items.date_display: By the early 15th century; first published 1881
items.region_display: England; witness dialect disputed between Northern (?) and a mixed western Midland analysis

items.date_display: Thirteenth-century composition; earliest securely dated direct witness 1272–1282
items.region_display: Medieval England; exact place of composition unknown

items.preferred_title: Zu dem ‘Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall’
items.date_display: Medieval poem attested by 1272–1282; scholarly note published 1881
items.region_display: Medieval England; no more precise place of composition is securely established

items.date_display: Fifteenth century; copied in Bodleian MS Bodley 423 by approximately 1430-1440
items.region_display: England; precise place of composition and dialect region unresolved

items.preferred_title: Complaint of a Prisoner against Fortune
items.date_display: Fifteenth century; surviving witnesses from the second half of the fifteenth century
items.region_display: England; exact place of composition unknown

items.date_display: English translation made after the French work of c. 1444 and attested by c. 1475–1500; article published 1911
items.region_display: Medieval England; no more precise place of translation is securely established

items.preferred_title: With an O and an I
items.date_display: Medieval texts chiefly attested in fourteenth- and fifteenth-century witnesses; edited selection published 1904
items.region_display: England; precise composition places vary or remain unresolved

items.date_display: Texts attested in fourteenth- and first-half-fifteenth-century English manuscripts; edited selection published 1904
items.region_display: Medieval texts transmitted in English manuscript witnesses; article compiled in Cambridge and published in Halle an der Saale

items.preferred_title: Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift
items.author_display: Anonymous authors or compilers of two verse works
items.date_display: Medieval composition dates unknown; X 90 main hand dated first quarter of the fifteenth century by MEG-C, with older late-fourteenth-century claims; edited 1895–1896 and published 1896
items.region_display: England; X 90 main hand localized to Norfolk as scribal language; edited in Gothenburg; exact composition places unknown

items.preferred_title: Rezepte, Segen und Zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer Handschriften
items.date_display: Source texts attested in a later-fourteenth- to fifteenth-century witness and a manuscript dated 1597; edited selection published 1897
items.region_display: Section I: South-East Midlands dialect, with most X 90 main-hand passages localized to Norfolk; Section II origin unknown

items.preferred_title: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English authors or compilers
items.date_display: Medieval components attested from 1272-1282 to the mid-fifteenth century; edited selection first published 1880; composition dates unknown
items.region_display: England; Cambridge witness language localized to Leicestershire; Cotton dialect unresolved; Digby collateral target language localized to north-west Gloucestershire

items.preferred_title: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
items.date_display: Composed by the early fourteenth century at the latest; first attested in a late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century witness
items.region_display: England; earliest known witness associated with the West Midlands

items.date_display: By the first half of the fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved; represented witness c. 1480
items.region_display: England; exact place of composition and dialect region unresolved

items.date_display: Early fifteenth century; surviving copies late sixteenth to early seventeenth century
items.region_display: England; London and Westminster setting

items.region_display: English source-song tradition; the principal Selden component was probably copied at Worcester Abbey, but individual composition places remain unresolved

items.date_display: Possibly before 1400; exact composition date and unity unresolved
items.region_display: England; Oxford is securely associated only with Fable VII's manuscript rubric

items.author_display: Benedict Burgh; attribution disputed for Parvus Cato
items.date_display: Cato Major probably composed or translated 1433–1440; Parvus Cato attribution and date unresolved
items.region_display: England; no narrower work-level place asserted

items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
items.date_display: Composed 1250-1290, with circa 1260 suggested; extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; Horstmann edition published 1874
items.region_display: England; the original dialect is only broadly southern and no precise place of composition is established

items.preferred_title: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)
items.date_display: Translation c.1438; surviving witness mid-fifteenth century; first known publication of this witness 1893
items.region_display: England; exact translation place unknown; Zupitza describes the Durham text's language as East Midland

items.date_display: Medieval texts chiefly fifteenth century; serial edition 1911-1913

items.date_display: Probably around 1400 or early fifteenth century; unique surviving witness approximately 1450; first edition 1910

items.date_display: By the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved; first known publication 1916
items.region_display: England; exact composition locality unknown. DIMEV indexes the Cambridge codex to East Anglia, but that witness-level localization is not treated as a composition place

items.preferred_title: A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306

items.date_display: The three manuscript components were copied circa 1465; the edited sequence was first published in 1880
items.region_display: England, with London specifically associated with the civic chronicle; exact place of compilation unknown

items.preferred_title: Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary
items.author_display: Anonymous; no shared or distinct authorship is established for the three romances
items.date_display: Works composed from the late fourteenth to early fifteenth century; collection first published 1842
items.region_display: Northern and northwestern England; the proposed origin varies by romance

items.date_display: Translated by 1444, probably close to production of the 1439-1444 dedication manuscript; exact composition date unresolved
items.region_display: England; exact place of translation and dialect region unestablished

items.date_display: Probably around 1400; surviving witnesses are later fifteenth-century copies; source edition 1886
items.region_display: Probably the Northeast Midlands of England; witness A was copied in a North-Midland context

items.preferred_title: Early English Poems and Lives of Saints
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English poets and compilers
items.date_display: Medieval texts attested from the thirteenth to mid-fifteenth century; editorial compilation published 1862
items.region_display: England and south-east Ireland; witness dialect regions include the south-west Midlands, Somerset, north Worcestershire, and Waterford

items.date_display: Probably late eleventh or early twelfth century; the sole surviving copy is late twelfth century
items.region_display: England; the witness has southwestern transitional linguistic features, but the work's exact place of translation is unknown

items.date_display: Circa 1400; unique surviving witness circa 1450; source edition 1888
items.region_display: Probably England; the surviving witness was produced in England

items.preferred_title: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle
items.date_display: First-version Chronicle completed in 1457; edited selection published 1 October 1912
items.region_display: England; exact place of composition is not securely established; the sole manuscript was produced in eastern England

items.date_display: Mid-fifteenth century according to Breul; first securely attested in late-fifteenth-century witnesses; source article published 1886
items.region_display: England, probably the Midlands according to Breul; witness A was copied about 1480 in a North-Midland context

items.author_display: Anonymous poets; article by Karl Breul
items.date_display: Medieval carols probably fifteenth century; earliest surviving witnesses early to mid-fifteenth century; both first printed in 1843; Breul article published 1890
items.region_display: Original dialect and region unlocalized; Breul notes northern features in the Annunciation carol, while the shared NLS witness was copied in a North-Midland context

items.preferred_title: Account of the Company of St. George in Norwich
items.author_display: Benjamin Mackerell, compiler and antiquarian transcriber; the medieval documentary creators are not individually identified
items.editor_display: Norfolk and Norwich Archaeological Society, with no personal editor named in the inspected article
items.date_display: Mackerell compilation dated 1737; represented records concern a disputed foundation date, 9 May 1417, and 27 March 1452; printed 1852
items.region_display: Norwich, Norfolk, England

items.preferred_title: Household Inventory of the Fifteenth Century
items.date_display: 15 May 1463; first published November 1852
items.region_display: Probably Little Baddow, Essex, England

items.preferred_title: Lord Grey de Ruthyn to Griffith ap David ap Griffith
items.author_display: Reginald Grey, Lord Grey de Ruthyn
items.editor_display: Francis Charles Hingeston; supporting excerpts transcribed by Henry Ellis
items.date_display: After 11 June and no later than 23 June 1400
items.region_display: The Welsh Marches, especially the Ruthin lordship, Chirkland, and Oswestry; exact place of composition unstated

items.preferred_title: The Earl of March to Henry IV
items.date_display: 18 February 1400
items.region_display: Dunbar, Scotland

items.preferred_title: An Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI
items.date_display: Dated 20 March 1432 (authenticity disputed); first known publication 1864
items.region_display: Cheshire, England (documentary associations); scribe possibly Cheshire or the Welsh border

items.date_display: Compilation no later than the fifteenth-century witness; individual sayings may be older; first known publication of this selection May 1854
items.region_display: England; exact compilation place unknown; Harley MS 3362 scribal language localized by DIMEV/LALME to Ely

items.preferred_title: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI
items.author_display: Anonymous (described as a servant of Edward IV)
items.date_display: 1471, after 26 May
items.region_display: England (Yorkist royal milieu; exact place unknown)

items.date_display: Composed by the early fourteenth century at the latest; first attested in a late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century witness

items.preferred_title: A Volume of English Miscellanies Illustrating the History and Language of the Northern Counties of England: CME00131 documentary selection

items.date_display: Fifteenth century, by 1496; first printed in 1496; Satchell composite edition 1883
items.region_display: England; exact place of composition unknown

items.date_display: Composed about 1250-1300; first attested in the Auchinleck Manuscript, 1331-1340
items.region_display: Kent (composition); represented witnesses have London, Shropshire, Dorset, and Lancashire associations

items.date_display: Probably composed or translated in the 1420s; traditionally dated c.1450; witness dated broadly to the fifteenth century by James and c.1450-75 by Parker
items.region_display: Probably London, England; the unique witness has an Essex dialect profile

items.preferred_title: The History of the Holy Grail
items.date_display: Translated in the 1420s; first attested c. 1450–1475; first verified publication 1861–1863
items.region_display: London, England; surviving-witness dialect localized to Essex

items.preferred_title: Early English Artistical Receipts (Wright's 1844 selection from Sloane MS 73)

items.preferred_title: Craft Recipes from Harley MS 2253, f. 52v
items.date_display: Composition unknown; the witness addition is dated in or soon after 1307 by Wright, ?a1350 by the MED, and by current codicological scholarship to Scribe C, probably not much later than the c.1330-40 main hand
items.region_display: Unknown; the codex's West Midlands and Ludlow attribution belongs principally to the main Harley scribe and is not transferred to Scribe C or to the recipes' composition

items.date_display: Distinct medieval works and version units; no single composition date established; edited selection published 1919
items.region_display: Unknown; witness origins and dialects are not transferred to the abstract works

items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English proverb makers and transmitters
items.date_display: Medieval passages attested in fifteenth-century Douce 52 and fourteenth- to fifteenth-century Bozon witnesses; editorial selection published 1906
items.region_display: England; exact composition places of the selected sayings are unknown

items.date_display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; first attested in two early-fifteenth-century witnesses; first verified publication in 1909
items.region_display: England; no place of composition is established, and the Northamptonshire localization of the first St John's scribe is retained only as witness-level dialect evidence

items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
items.date_display: Composed 1250-1290, with circa 1260 suggested; extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; Bälz edition published 1909
items.region_display: England; the original dialect is only broadly southern and no precise place of composition is established

items.preferred_title: Life and Translation of Saint Thomas of Canterbury
items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English poet or poets and translator or translators
items.date_display: Life composed approximately 1275; Translation present by about 1300; critical edition published 1919
items.region_display: England, with a qualified South-West Midlands literary and dialectal association

items.preferred_title: Life of Saint Cecilia: six comparative streams

items.date_display: Not securely established; attested in fifteenth-century Sloane MS 1986; first printed in 1862

items.date_display: Around 1420; sole surviving witness from the first quarter of the fifteenth century; first published in 1830

items.preferred_title: The South English Ministry and Passion (Laud Misc. 108 Fragment)

items.date_display: Mappula circa 1440; almanac composed 1445; both attested in a manuscript cataloged circa 1450; both printed in 1887, explicitly for the first time in the case of Mappula

items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English poet-adapters; The Sayings is traditionally associated with Bernard of Clairvaux

items.region_display: England; exact places of composition are unknown, and Laud Hand A's West Oxfordshire scribal dialect is retained only as witness-level evidence

items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English translator; Guy de Chauliac is the attributed author of the larger source text

items.author_display: Anonymous; formerly attributed to William of Nassyngton

items.region_display: Yorkshire composition; the represented manuscript's first scribe is localized more narrowly to northwest Yorkshire, a witness-level dialect claim

items.region_display: England; the exact place of composition is not established in this record

items.region_display: England; associated with late-medieval London literary language; exact place of composition uncertain

items.date_display: By the conjectural [1507?] first printing; exact composition date unknown

items.author_display: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter

items.preferred_title: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian
